title: The Fifth Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## March 29, 2020 — "The Fifth Sunday in Lent"

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fifth Sunday in Lent, also called Passion Sunday, occurring two weeks before Easter (April 12, 2020). The RCL readings for this Sunday typically include Ezekiel 37:1-14 (the valley of dry bones), Romans 6:9-11 (Christ's resurrection power), and John 11:1-45 (the raising of Lazarus). The sermon falls during Lent's final stretch, a season of penitence and preparation for the Passion and Resurrection, with themes of death, restoration, and the power of Christ's redemptive work particularly resonant.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church and global Anglican Communion were in crisis mode regarding pandemic protocols. The House of Bishops issued guidance on March 28-29, 2020, directing suspension of in-person worship and recommending online services, with this guidance arriving essentially as this sermon was being preached. The Diocese of Colorado was implementing emergency closures of in-person services; this may have been among the first Sundays when worship shifted entirely online or was severely restricted at Saint John's Cathedral.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver had entered mandatory stay-at-home orders on March 24, 2020, with Colorado Governor Jared Polis extending the order through April 11. The Denver area was experiencing rapid COVID-19 case increases, with hospitals beginning to strain. Saint John's Cathedral, located on Logan Street in downtown Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood, would have been closed to public worship. Local news was dominated by unemployment claims, food bank demands, and the surreal emptiness of typically busy downtown areas.

NATIONAL NEWS

The United States was in the acute early phase of the COVID-19 pandemic, with over 120,000 confirmed cases and death tolls accelerating daily (approximately 2,000 deaths by March 29). President Trump's messaging remained inconsistent; he had declared a national emergency on March 13 but continued downplaying severity. Congress was negotiating the CARES Act (passed March 27, providing $2 trillion in pandemic relief). Sports leagues remained suspended, unemployment was spiking toward historic levels, and hospitals in New York City were overwhelmed.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Global confirmed COVID-19 cases exceeded 750,000 with over 36,000 deaths. Italy was in the depths of its catastrophic first wave, with Madrid's hospitals similarly overwhelmed. The United Kingdom was preparing for lockdown (officially announced March 23). Spain, France, and Germany were reporting exponential case increases. The World Health Organization was warning of global healthcare system collapse.

CULTURAL MOMENT

America was in profound collective shock and fear, with widespread uncertainty about pandemic duration, severity, and basic survival needs. Churches nationwide were experiencing unprecedented closures; the virtual pivot was chaotic and emotionally destabilizing for faith communities. Themes of isolation, mortality, loss of normalcy, and existential questioning dominated the national consciousness. Paradoxically, the lectionary's reading about raising Lazarus from death held raw, almost unbearable contemporary resonance for a congregation facing a mortality crisis with no foreseeable end.
